refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation approach) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each concern.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, exclusion zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite model data for access by planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, positioning) and store in a unified geometric representation (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with feature classification metadata; expose via standardized query interface.

DP2: Decompose worksite into logical zones (safety perimeters, operational areas, hazard regions) and represent each zone as a parameterized geometric primitive (polygon, polyhedron) with associated feature attributes; maintain zone registry and spatial index.

DP3: Ingest pre-existing site documentation (CAD, survey data, safety plans) as authoritative baseline; overlay real-time sensor observations as delta layers; reconcile conflicts via confidence scoring and temporal versioning.

DP4: Implement a feature-agnostic spatial database (graph or relational) that stores discrete observations (point, edge, surface) with provenance (sensor type, timestamp, confidence); allow dynamic feature inference via post-processing rules.

DP5: Establish a hierarchical spatial model where coarse features (building footprints, access roads) are fixed; fine features (obstacles, hazards) are dynamic; separate storage and update cadence by feature stability class.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP5 (hybrid): Decompose worksite into logical safety/operational zones as parameterized geometric primitives with feature classification; implement a hierarchical spatial model stratified by feature stability (fixed baseline vs. dynamic observations). Expose via a zone-centric query interface that supports both static safety constraints and real-time hazard updates. This balances representational clarity (zones align with safety/operational policies) with architectural independence (fixed and dynamic layers can be updated asynchronously).

potential_sources: Tight coupling between zone geometry and safety policy interpretation: if safety rules change, zone definitions may require rework. Mitigation: parameterize zone rules separately from geometry.
Dependency on sensor data quality and calibration: if sensor accuracy degrades, feature classification confidence drops. Mitigation: explicit confidence metadata and fallback to baseline.
Hierarchical stratification assumes clear stability boundaries; ambiguous features (e.g., temporary obstacles) may blur layers. Mitigation: define explicit feature classification taxonomy upfront.

Query interface must support both spatial (geometric) and semantic (feature type, safety class) predicates; poor interface design couples consumers to internal representation. Mitigation: define abstract query contract early.
coupling_check.notes: DP2+DP5 avoids technology lock-in (sensor type, storage backend) and supports independent evolution of baseline and dynamic layers. Zone-centric decomposition aligns with operational and safety domains, reducing cognitive load. Risk: over-parameterization of zones may create brittleness if worksite topology is highly irregular.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite features can be meaningfully classified into fixed (baseline) and dynamic (real-time) categories. Risk: If feature stability is context-dependent or highly variable, stratification strategy may fail. Mitigation: conduct feature stability analysis during requirements refinement.

Assumption: Safety plan and operational policies are stable enough to drive zone definitions. Risk: If policies change frequently, zone model becomes a maintenance burden. Mitigation: decouple zone geometry from policy rules; store rules separately.

Assumption: Baseline site data (CAD, survey) is available and reasonably accurate. Risk: If baseline is missing or stale, model initialization is delayed or unreliable. Mitigation: define fallback initialization (e.g., sensor-only bootstrap).

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can consume a zone-centric model. Risk: If consumers expect raw sensor data or different spatial abstractions, interface mismatch occurs. Mitigation: validate consumer requirements early; design query interface iteratively.

Assumption: Spatial indexing and query performance are acceptable for real-time planning. Risk: If worksite is very large or feature density is high, query latency may violate planning cycle time. Mitigation: prototype with representative data; consider spatial partitioning strategies.

Assumption: Feature classification taxonomy is well-defined and stable. Risk: If new feature types emerge during operation, taxonomy extension may require model migration. Mitigation: design taxonomy as extensible schema; version the model.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) which features are fixed vs. dynamic (e.g., building footprints are fixed; debris/hazards are dynamic); (2) required query latency and spatial resolution for planning/execution; (3) acceptable confidence thresholds for feature classification; (4) whether baseline site data is available and in what format. This will reduce ambiguity in DP selection and enable earlier validation of the zone-centric model against consumer needs. Additionally, define a feature classification taxonomy and stability matrix as part of the design contract to avoid late-stage rework.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial modeling, zone decomposition, hazard classification, and constraint enforcement. Owns the hierarchical spatial model (fixed baseline geometry + dynamic observation layer), zone-centric query interface, and real-time hazard update logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives dynamic sensor observations (position, obstacle detections, environmental conditions) to update the dynamic observation layer of the spatial model.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits zone-based safety constraints, hazard classifications, and real-time alerts derived from zone queries and feature stability analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational task context to inform zone relevance and safety constraint prioritization for the current mission phase.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes fixed baseline geometry and dynamic observation updates across distributed agents; enables asynchronous layer updates.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of spatial safety modeling, zone decomposition, hazard classification, and constraint enforcement. The hierarchical spatial model (fixed + dynamic layers) aligns with safety policy representation. Zone-centric queries are core to safety decision-making. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (sensor input), SafetyAlert (output), Tasking (context), and Network Link (sync) cross the boundary—all expected and well-defined.

Poor fit. Vehicle is responsible for motion control and state management, not spatial modeling or safety policy. Placing zone decomposition and hazard classification in Vehicle would couple safety logic tightly to vehicle-specific concerns, making it difficult to reuse the spatial model across multiple agents or update safety policies independently. High diffusion: Safety Services, Teleoperation, and external safety systems would need to query Vehicle for zone information, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit. Teleoperation manages remote operator interaction and command translation, not spatial modeling or hazard reasoning. Embedding zone decomposition and constraint enforcement in Teleoperation would conflate user interface concerns with safety logic, making it impossible to enforce safety constraints autonomously or across multiple control modes. High diffusion: Safety Services, Vehicle, and autonomous decision-making would all need to replicate or query zone logic.

Moderate-to-poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system integrator. While it could host the spatial model as a shared service, doing so would blur the boundary between system integration and safety-critical domain logic. Safety Services is a more specific, cohesive owner. Placing the DP in NGHE Solution would increase coupling to the overall system architecture and make safety logic harder to test, version, and evolve independently.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services owns the hierarchical spatial model (fixed baseline + dynamic observation layers), zone decomposition logic, feature classification, and the zone-centric query interface. It receives Telemetry (sensor updates), Tasking (operational context), and Network Link (baseline sync), and emits SafetyAlert (constraints and hazard notifications). This placement ensures that safety-critical spatial reasoning is centralized, independently testable, and decoupled from vehicle control, teleoperation, or system integration concerns. The asynchronous layer architecture (fixed vs. dynamic) is naturally supported by Safety Services' ability to manage independent update cycles and expose a stable query interface.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services becomes a bottleneck for real-time hazard updates, latency in zone queries could delay safety alerts. Mitigation: Implement efficient spatial indexing (e.g., quadtree, octree) and cache frequently queried zones.

Risk: Multiple agents or vehicles may need independent spatial models (e.g., different baseline geometries for different worksites). Mitigation: Design Safety Services to support parameterized zone definitions and multi-instance baseline management, or introduce a lightweight spatial model abstraction that can be instantiated per agent.

Risk: Dynamic observation layer updates from Telemetry may arrive out-of-order or with variable latency. Mitigation: Implement temporal consistency checks and conflict resolution strategies in the dynamic layer update logic.

Risk: Tasking context changes may require rapid re-evaluation of zone relevance and constraint priorities. Mitigation: Ensure zone-centric query interface supports efficient filtering and re-ranking based on task state.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) alignment with worksite safety plan and operational policies. Safety Services as the IM owner can demonstrate model generation, feature classification, zone decomposition, and exposure via zone-centric query interface. Real-time hazard updates and constraint enforcement are observable through SafetyAlert emissions and Telemetry ingestion.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The allocation from FR to DP to IM is clear and traceable: FR requires machine-readable spatial representation → DP specifies hierarchical zones with feature stability stratification and zone-centric queries → IM (Safety Services) owns the spatial model, zone decomposition, hazard classification, and query interface. Ownership boundaries are well-defined; external interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are explicit and necessary. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for which aspect of the FR.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP (hybrid DP2 + DP5) directly addresses the FR. Decomposing the worksite into logical safety/operational zones as parameterized geometric primitives satisfies 'capture and represent worksite spatial features.' The hierarchical stratification (fixed baseline vs. dynamic observations) ensures the model is machine-readable and supports both static safety constraints and real-time updates. The zone-centric query interface provides the mechanism for planning/execution functions to access the model. The DP balances representational clarity (zones align with safety/operational policies per acceptance criteria) with architectural independence (asynchronous layer updates). No logical gaps between requirement and solution concept.

diffusion_risks: Latency diffusion: If Safety Services becomes a bottleneck for real-time hazard updates, zone query latency could delay SafetyAlert emissions. Mitigation: Implement efficient spatial indexing (quadtree/octree) and zone caching. Acceptance criterion tie-in: 'available to planning/execution functions' requires sub-100ms query response for safety-critical zones.

Multi-instance diffusion: If multiple worksites or agents require independent spatial models with different baseline geometries, Safety Services must support parameterized zone definitions and multi-instance baseline management. Risk: Baseline sync via Network Link could become complex if instances diverge. Mitigation: Design Safety Services with instance isolation and version-aware baseline synchronization.

Temporal consistency diffusion: Dynamic observation layer updates from Telemetry may arrive out-of-order or with variable latency, risking stale hazard classifications. Mitigation: Implement temporal consistency checks and conflict resolution in the dynamic layer update logic. Acceptance criterion tie-in: 'real-time hazard updates' requires bounded staleness guarantees.

Task-driven re-evaluation diffusion: Tasking context changes may require rapid re-evaluation of zone relevance and constraint priorities. Risk: If zone-centric query interface does not support efficient filtering/re-ranking, Tasking-driven updates could cause latency spikes. Mitigation: Ensure query interface supports task-aware zone filtering and priority re-ranking without full model re-computation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural, cohesive owner of the hierarchical spatial model, zone decomposition, feature classification, and zone-centric query interface. The DP is not scattered: all core logic (zone decomposition, hazard classification, constraint enforcement, dynamic layer updates) resides in Safety Services. External interfaces are minimal and well-justified: Telemetry (sensor input), SafetyAlert (output), Tasking (context), Network Link (baseline sync). No diffusion into Vehicle (motion control), Teleoperation (operator interaction), or NGHE Solution (system integration). The asynchronous layer architecture (fixed baseline + dynamic observations) is naturally supported by Safety Services' ability to manage independent update cycles. Risk of diffusion is low if spatial indexing and query efficiency are prioritized in design.

recommended_revisions: Specify spatial indexing strategy (e.g., quadtree for 2D, octree for 3D) and query latency SLA (e.g., <100ms for safety-critical zones) to mitigate latency diffusion risk and ensure 'available to planning/execution functions' acceptance criterion is met.

Define baseline geometry versioning and multi-instance management protocol in Safety Services to support multiple worksites/agents without diffusion of baseline sync logic into Network Link or external modules.

Establish temporal consistency model for dynamic observation layer: define acceptable staleness bounds, conflict resolution strategy (e.g., last-write-wins, consensus), and out-of-order update handling to ensure 'real-time hazard updates' criterion is verifiable.

Design zone-centric query interface to support task-aware filtering and priority re-ranking (e.g., 'zones relevant to current task phase') to prevent Tasking-driven re-evaluation from causing latency spikes or diffusing task logic into Safety Services.

Document the boundary between 'fixed baseline geometry' (owned by Safety Services, synchronized via Network Link) and 'dynamic observations' (updated via Telemetry) to clarify which layer is authoritative for different types of features (e.g., static obstacles vs. moving hazards) and prevent ambiguity in hazard classification.

Add acceptance criterion: 'Safety Services exposes zone-centric query interface that supports both static safety constraints (from worksite safety plan) and real-time hazard updates (from Telemetry) with documented latency and consistency guarantees.' This makes the DP-IM mapping verifiable against the FR.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gate results show strong pass marks across all three gates (fr_im_gate, fr_dp_gate, dp_im_gate) with excellent allocation clarity (0.92 containment score) and high verification feasibility. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical design decisions that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical acceptance criteria are met. Specifically: (1) spatial indexing and query latency SLA are non-negotiable for 'available to planning/execution functions' criterion; (2) baseline versioning and multi-instance management require explicit FR-level treatment to prevent diffusion; (3) temporal consistency and staleness bounds are safety-critical and must be elevated to sub-FR level; (4) task-aware query filtering requires explicit functional decomposition to prevent latency spikes. These are not minor implementation details—they are functional requirements that must be traced through DP and IM layers. Proceeding to next-level decomposition ensures these safety-critical concerns are architecturally owned and verifiable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 1.1: Provide sub-100ms zone-centric query interface for safety-critical spatial features (static obstacles, hazard zones, constraint boundaries) to support real-time planning and execution decisions.

FR 1.2: Maintain temporal consistency and bounded staleness (<500ms) in dynamic observation layer (moving hazards, environmental changes) to ensure real-time hazard updates are authoritative and conflict-free.
FR 1.3: Support parameterized baseline geometry versioning and multi-instance spatial model management to enable independent worksite/agent configurations without baseline sync diffusion.
FR 1.4: Enable task-aware zone filtering and priority re-ranking in query interface to support Tasking-driven context changes without latency degradation or task logic diffusion into Safety Services.

FR 1.5: Classify and stratify spatial features by stability (fixed baseline vs. dynamic observations) and assign authoritative ownership (Safety Services baseline vs. Telemetry-driven updates) to prevent ambiguity in hazard classification.

revise.why: The current DP (hybrid DP2 + DP5: hierarchical zones with feature stability stratification and zone-centric queries) is logically sound but lacks specificity on critical design parameters: (1) spatial indexing algorithm (quadtree/octree) and query optimization strategy are not explicitly named; (2) temporal consistency model (conflict resolution, staleness bounds, out-of-order handling) is not defined; (3) baseline versioning protocol and multi-instance isolation mechanism are not detailed; (4) task-aware filtering logic and priority re-ranking algorithm are not specified. These must be elevated to explicit DP sub-parameters to ensure the DP-IM mapping is verifiable and to guide Safety Services implementation. Recommend revising DP to include: DP1.1 (spatial indexing via quadtree/octree with <100ms query SLA), DP1.2 (temporal consistency via conflict resolution + staleness bounds), DP1.3 (baseline versioning + multi-instance isolation), DP1.4 (task-aware query filtering), DP1.5 (feature stratification and ownership assignment). This ensures each sub-FR has a corresponding DP that is architecturally traceable to Safety Services IM.

assumptions: Safety Services has sufficient computational capacity to support sub-100ms query latency for safety-critical zones without external caching layers.
Telemetry ingestion rate and latency are bounded such that dynamic observation layer can maintain <500ms staleness guarantee.
Baseline geometry is stable enough to support versioning without requiring continuous re-synchronization across multiple instances.
Tasking context changes are infrequent enough that task-aware query re-ranking does not require real-time re-computation of zone priorities.
Network Link provides reliable baseline synchronization with acceptable latency for multi-instance consistency.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized in a way that can be encoded as zone constraints and feature classifications.
open_risks: Latency diffusion: If spatial indexing is not optimized (e.g., linear scan instead of quadtree), query latency could exceed 100ms SLA, violating 'available to planning/execution functions' criterion. Mitigation: Specify indexing algorithm and benchmark query performance in DP1.1.

Temporal consistency diffusion: Out-of-order Telemetry updates or variable ingestion latency could cause stale hazard classifications. Mitigation: Define conflict resolution strategy and staleness bounds in DP1.2; implement temporal consistency checks in Safety Services.

Multi-instance baseline sync diffusion: If baseline versioning is not properly isolated, changes in one worksite instance could propagate to others, causing safety violations. Mitigation: Design instance isolation and version-aware synchronization in DP1.3.

Task-driven re-evaluation latency: If task-aware filtering is not efficient, Tasking-driven context changes could cause query latency spikes. Mitigation: Implement pre-computed task-zone relevance maps or incremental re-ranking in DP1.4.

Feature ownership ambiguity: If boundary between fixed baseline and dynamic observations is not clearly defined, hazard classification could be inconsistent (e.g., moving obstacle classified as static). Mitigation: Establish authoritative ownership assignment in DP1.5.

Safety-critical acceptance criterion verification: Current acceptance criteria ('available to planning/execution functions', 'real-time hazard updates') are qualitative. Quantitative SLAs (latency, staleness, consistency) must be defined to make verification feasible.
evidence_needed: Spatial indexing performance benchmarks: latency vs. zone count, query complexity, update frequency for quadtree/octree implementations.
Telemetry ingestion profile: expected update rate, latency distribution, out-of-order frequency to inform temporal consistency model design.
Baseline geometry stability analysis: frequency of baseline changes, multi-instance divergence patterns, synchronization overhead.
Task-aware query filtering efficiency: latency impact of task-context filtering, pre-computation vs. on-demand re-ranking trade-offs.
Feature classification taxonomy: mapping of worksite safety plan constraints to zone features, authoritative ownership assignment (baseline vs. dynamic).
Acceptance criterion quantification: stakeholder agreement on latency SLA (<100ms), staleness bounds (<500ms), consistency guarantees (conflict resolution strategy).
Safety Services architectural design: spatial indexing implementation, temporal consistency logic, baseline versioning protocol, task-aware query interface specification.
